title: Not With My Pig You Dont

jokes.text: What is the highest recognition a self-made Black professional can receive? A raise!
jokes.setup: Philip asks a rhetorical question about professional recognition
jokes.punchline: Will cuts through the pretense with a mercenary answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Will's street-smart cynicism vs. Philip's idealism. Clean setup/punchline structure with good economy.

jokes.text: Is it a cash award?
jokes.setup: Philip announces his prestigious award
jokes.punchline: Hilary immediately reduces it to financial terms
jokes.explanation: Classic Hilary - reducing everything to material value. Economical and character-appropriate but predictable.

jokes.text: Hilary, don't be so shortsighted. This award is an honor which leads to prestige... which leads to cash.
jokes.setup: Philip criticizes Hilary for being materialistic
jokes.punchline: He immediately reveals his own materialistic motivation
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic structure - Philip's self-righteous setup undercut by his own shallow ending. The pause before 'cash' sells it.

jokes.text: The only time you're on the streets is when you're waiting for the valet to bring your car around.
jokes.setup: Philip boasts about helping 'brothers and sisters on the streets'
jokes.punchline: Will points out Philip's actual relationship to 'the streets'
jokes.explanation: Sharp class commentary with perfect specificity. The valet detail makes it visual and cuts deep. Classic Will vs. Philip dynamic.

jokes.text: Seems to me every place you go, trouble follows.
jokes.setup: Philip lists his civil rights activism locations
jokes.punchline: Will reframes it as Philip causing the trouble
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - takes Philip's proud historical recounting and flips the causation. Perfect economy and timing.

jokes.text: All that and he still found time to vote for Reagan. Twice.
jokes.setup: Vivian lists Philip's liberal accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Will points out his conservative voting record
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire highlighting class/race contradictions. The 'twice' adds perfect emphasis. Brave for network TV.

jokes.text: Reporter? Photographer? New dress.
jokes.setup: Philip mentions press coverage
jokes.punchline: Hilary's immediate materialistic calculation
jokes.explanation: Classic Hilary logic chain - perfectly economical character beat. The mathematical simplicity works.

jokes.text: Daddy, this is wonderful. Two new dresses.
jokes.setup: Philip mentions both interview and banquet
jokes.punchline: Hilary doubles her dress calculation
jokes.explanation: Predictable escalation of her earlier calculation, but character-appropriate and economical.

jokes.text: Great. Grandma and Grandpa are coming to visit. Toys.
jokes.setup: Family discusses grandparents visiting for awards ceremony
jokes.punchline: Ashley reduces it to gift expectations
jokes.explanation: Perfect child logic - cutting straight to what matters to her. The single word 'toys' is economical and honest.

jokes.text: Come on, Ash. Less like George Michael, more like Hammer. Come on.
jokes.setup: Will is teaching Ashley to dance
jokes.punchline: He critiques her style using pop culture references
jokes.explanation: Era-specific pop culture reference showing Will's street credibility vs. Ashley's whiteness. Works for the time period.

jokes.text: That's it. Baby, you got to put some more butt into it.
jokes.setup: Will coaching Ashley's dancing
jokes.punchline: His blunt, street-style instruction
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate coaching style that shows cultural clash. Physical comedy element implied.

jokes.text: I don't know, Hattie. He don't look like the devil incarnate.
jokes.setup: Grandparents meet Will for the first time
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's dry assessment contradicts Philip's warnings
jokes.explanation: Great character introduction - shows Grandpa's wit and hints at Philip's exaggerated complaints about Will.

jokes.text: Zeke? ... Hey, Uncle Zeke.
jokes.setup: Grandma calls Philip 'Zeke'
jokes.punchline: Will's delighted reaction to this revelation
jokes.explanation: Will's obvious glee at discovering ammunition against Philip. The name 'Zeke' perfectly contrasts with 'Philip's' sophistication.

jokes.text: Am I tripping, or does this remind you of the last episode of Family Ties?
jokes.setup: Family reunion scene with parents surprising sophisticated son
jokes.punchline: Will breaks fourth wall comparing to another sitcom
jokes.explanation: Rare meta moment - acknowledging the sitcom trope they're playing out. Bold choice that works because it's Will being observant.

jokes.text: When you sent that money, me and my buddy Leroy went down to the dry goods store and we each got a suit, a fishing rod, and some radial tires.
jokes.setup: Philip asks about the suit he paid for
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's creative budgeting priorities
jokes.explanation: Perfect old-school practicality - why buy one expensive thing when you can buy multiple useful things? The specificity sells it.

jokes.text: One pound of lard, four handfuls poke greens, and a big old slab of fatback. Now, if it comes to more than $1, they're cheating you.
jokes.setup: Grandma gives Geoffrey a shopping list
jokes.punchline: The hilariously outdated prices
jokes.explanation: Classic generation gap humor - Grandma's Depression-era pricing vs. 1990s reality. The $1 limit makes it absurd.

jokes.text: Have mercy!
jokes.setup: Grandma describes the Southern meal she's planning
jokes.punchline: Will's excited reaction using his catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Standard Will catchphrase usage. Works because it shows his genuine excitement about home cooking.

jokes.text: He would strip down to his birthday suit and dive right in.
jokes.setup: Grandma reminisces about young Philip at swimming holes
jokes.punchline: TMI about his naked swimming habits
jokes.explanation: Standard embarrassing parent story. The 'birthday suit' euphemism keeps it family-friendly while maintaining the cringe.

jokes.text: He just loved his skinny-dipping, him and Melvin.
jokes.setup: More stories about Philip's swimming companion
jokes.punchline: Sets up the reveal that Melvin is his pig
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the Melvin reveal. The audience assumes Melvin is human, making the pig revelation funnier.

jokes.text: So, Melvin was like his best friend? Melvin was his pig.
jokes.setup: Will asks about Philip's 'friend' Melvin
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Melvin was actually a pig
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection payoff. The audience shares Will's assumption, making the reveal genuinely surprising. Economical and devastating to Philip's image.

jokes.text: Are any of them humiliating stories? The best ones are.
jokes.setup: Will asks about Philip stories
jokes.punchline: Grandma confirms the most embarrassing ones are best
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing both Will's mischievous nature and Grandma's honesty about having embarrassing stories.

jokes.text: How did you see me? Mama knows.
jokes.setup: Will is caught sneaking in late
jokes.punchline: Grandma's mystical explanation for catching him
jokes.explanation: Universal parent/grandparent trope - the mysterious ability to know everything. Economical and relatable.

jokes.text: How much is a little? Let's just say I went out after I was supposed to be in.
jokes.setup: Grandma asks how late Will was
jokes.punchline: Will's circular non-answer
jokes.explanation: Classic evasive teenager logic. The wordplay of explaining lateness by restating lateness is mildly clever.

jokes.text: I don't know what you all got that Geoffrey for.
jokes.setup: Grandma is up early making breakfast
jokes.punchline: Her dismissive attitude toward Geoffrey's usefulness
jokes.explanation: Shows Grandma's self-reliant attitude and subtle class commentary about hired help. Character-appropriate but predictable.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil's adopted, right?
jokes.setup: Will observes how different Philip is from his parents
jokes.punchline: His logical but wrong conclusion about adoption
jokes.explanation: Will's street-smart logic applied to family dynamics. The contrast between Philip and his parents is so stark it seems reasonable.

jokes.text: Won it four years running. They had to retire his slop bucket.
jokes.setup: Philip won pig-raising trophies multiple years
jokes.punchline: His slop bucket being retired like a sports jersey
jokes.explanation: Brilliant sports metaphor applied to pig farming. Treating a slop bucket like a retired jersey number is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Slop bucket? I can't wait till he wakes up.
jokes.setup: Learning about Philip's pig-farming accolades
jokes.punchline: Will's obvious delight at this ammunition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Will reaction - his mischievous anticipation is palpable. Sets up the coming confrontation.

jokes.text: They told him to leave, but little Zeke just stood his ground.
jokes.setup: Story about Philip entering whites-only restroom
jokes.punchline: Framing it as brave civil rights stance
jokes.explanation: Sets up the later revelation that it wasn't political - he just really had to pee. Good misdirection.

jokes.text: What is this, the von Trapp family?
jokes.setup: Family rehearsing coordinated responses for reporter
jokes.punchline: Will compares them to Sound of Music family
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference highlighting the artificial nature of their rehearsed responses. Will as outside observer.

jokes.text: Sorry, Dad. All of Grandma's pies last night and waffles this morning. I just crashed. I've got the sugar blues. I'm this close to a crying jag.
jokes.setup: Carlton fell asleep during rehearsal
jokes.punchline: His dramatic explanation about sugar overload
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton over-explanation with medical terminology. 'Sugar blues' and 'crying jag' are wonderfully specific.

jokes.text: Your father sent a picture in already. Am I in it? Yes, you are. Am I pretty?
jokes.setup: Reporter mentions existing photo
jokes.punchline: Hilary's immediate vanity concerns
jokes.explanation: Classic Hilary - immediate self-interest. The escalation from 'am I in it' to 'am I pretty' is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Well, I really don't like to blow my own horn. Growing up on the mean streets of Baltimore, Maryland...
jokes.setup: Philip claims humility about self-promotion
jokes.punchline: Immediately launches into grandiose self-promotion
jokes.explanation: Classic hypocrisy setup - claiming not to brag while immediately bragging extensively. The contradiction is perfect.

jokes.text: remained active in the field of civil rights culminating in his crowning achievement in 1975 when he was elected to the board of the ASPCA.
jokes.setup: Ashley recites Philip's civil rights achievements
jokes.punchline: Confuses NAACP with ASPCA (animal protection)
jokes.explanation: Great malapropism that deflates the whole rehearsed speech. The animal rights/civil rights confusion is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: ASPCA?
jokes.setup: Ashley tries to mention Philip's civil rights work
jokes.punchline: She confuses NAACP with animal welfare organization
jokes.explanation: Child's malapropism that inadvertently makes Philip sound ridiculous. The reporter's confused reaction sells it.

jokes.text: After living in Bel-Air, it was great to spend time at Disneyland to get back to reality.
jokes.setup: Will talking about returning to reality
jokes.punchline: Claims Disneyland is more real than Bel-Air
jokes.explanation: Brilliant commentary on how artificial wealthy life is - that even a theme park feels more real. Perfect Will observation.

jokes.text: Dull? Sweetheart, that's one thing my uncle is not. Max out, lady. I got a couple of stories for you.
jokes.setup: Will defends Philip against 'dull' accusation
jokes.punchline: His confidence that he has better stories
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's turning point where Will's good intentions backfire. His '90s slang ('max out') dates it perfectly.

jokes.text: Madam, the press is made up of a very low type of person. You see what they've done to Fergie.
jokes.setup: Explaining how embarrassing stories got published
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's British perspective on tabloid treatment of royalty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey - bringing British sensibility and royal gossip knowledge to explain American tabloid culture.

jokes.text: Known to his barnyard companions as Zeke, Banks is no stranger to accolades. This ex-hog-handler slopped his way to the top with the help of his trusted companion Melvin the pig.
jokes.setup: Philip reading the newspaper article about himself
jokes.punchline: The embarrassing agricultural language describing his success
jokes.explanation: Brilliant writing - 'slopped his way to the top' is perfect wordplay connecting his pig past to his success. Devastating to his image.

jokes.text: Hey, Uncle Zeke. I got your 100 black and whites.
jokes.setup: Philip ordered 100 copies of what he expected to be a positive article
jokes.punchline: Will's cheerful delivery of the embarrassing newspapers
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - Will's oblivious cheerfulness contrasts with Philip's horror. The 'Zeke' usage twists the knife.

jokes.text: Will, honey... run.
jokes.setup: Philip's rage is building toward Will
jokes.punchline: Grandma's calm, practical advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect grandmotherly wisdom - delivered with calm authority. The economy of 'run' makes it funnier than a longer explanation.

jokes.text: Where I come from they call that being ashamed... Mr. Big Shot!
jokes.setup: Philip claims he's not ashamed of his background
jokes.punchline: Grandma calls him out directly with sarcastic title
jokes.explanation: Grandma cutting through Philip's BS with rural directness. 'Mr. Big Shot' drips with sarcasm and motherly disappointment.

jokes.text: We don't want to be a bother. We'll just hang on to these empty plates.
jokes.setup: Waiter offers food at the banquet
jokes.punchline: Grandma's martyrdom act
jokes.explanation: Perfect passive-aggressive parent behavior. The image of holding empty plates while refusing food is wonderfully petty.

jokes.text: We don't want to impose. We'll just fade into the background.
jokes.setup: Continuing the martyrdom performance
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's dramatic self-effacement
jokes.explanation: Continues the passive-aggressive performance. Less original than Grandma's version but maintains the family guilt trip.

jokes.text: Got any okra casserole?
jokes.setup: Fancy banquet with sophisticated food
jokes.punchline: Grandpa asking for his preferred rural dish
jokes.explanation: Simple culture clash humor - rural tastes in fancy setting. The specificity of okra casserole makes it authentic.

jokes.text: To hear him tell it, he just sprung full-grown from the ground and we had nothing to do with it.
jokes.setup: Philip's partners asking about parents
jokes.punchline: Grandma's description of Philip's denial of his origins
jokes.explanation: Vivid metaphor capturing Philip's shame about his background. The 'sprung from the ground' image is both funny and poignant.

jokes.text: Yes, he just sprung up like a big old eggplant.
jokes.setup: Grandma's 'sprung from ground' metaphor
jokes.punchline: Grandpa adds the vegetable comparison
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Grandma's metaphor. 'Big old eggplant' is both funny and slightly insulting to Philip.

jokes.text: You're the hog-slopping lawyer. I saw your picture in the paper this morning. Is Melvin going to be here tonight?
jokes.setup: Stranger recognizes Philip from the embarrassing article
jokes.punchline: Asking about his pig like it's a celebrity guest
jokes.explanation: Shows the public consequences of the embarrassing article. Treating the pig like a celebrity is appropriately absurd.

jokes.text: No, Melvin eschews these type of events. I'm the same way.
jokes.setup: Stranger asks about Melvin attending
jokes.punchline: Philip's pretentious response treating the pig as sophisticated
jokes.explanation: Perfect Philip response - using sophisticated vocabulary ('eschews') to dignify his pig. The final line shows his own discomfort.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil, your plaid golf shorts make you look like a fool. Those stories make you look great.
jokes.setup: Philip complains about looking foolish
jokes.punchline: Will's perspective on what actually makes him look foolish
jokes.explanation: Great contrast - Will pointing out that Philip's actual fashion choices are more embarrassing than his heroic past.

jokes.text: I mean, look, Uncle Phil, it's just like Rosa Parks. When she sat in the front of the bus, she wasn't doing it to prove a point either. She did it 'cause her feet hurt.
jokes.setup: Philip says his restroom incident wasn't about civil rights
jokes.punchline: Will's comparison to Rosa Parks' practical motivation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant historical parallel that reframes heroism in practical terms. Shows how ordinary people become heroes through circumstance.

jokes.text: Will, there's something you have to learn. Sometimes parents just don't understand.
jokes.setup: Philip discussing his relationship with his parents
jokes.punchline: References Will Smith's hit song title
jokes.explanation: Meta-reference to Will Smith's real song. Works both as genuine advice and as a wink to the audience about the actor's music career.

jokes.text: I hope he mentions his pig.
jokes.setup: Philip giving heartfelt speech about his parents
jokes.punchline: Will's focus on the embarrassing detail
jokes.explanation: Perfect Will moment - even during Philip's emotional speech, he's still fixated on the pig story. Character-consistent ending.

tmdb_overview: Will tries to smooth things between Philip and his very rural parents when they visit to see him receive an award.
